pages.raw_text:
The spokesnan-Revlew gave
frat page prominence yesterday
morning to the following communi'
cation:


To the Editor Of the Spokesman.
Review: l notice that j. T. ONO
in his campaign Is claiming the
support OF labor, both organized
and u?organized


l fail to see upon what grounds
he bases his claim. He certainly
can not base it upon his past rec
ord. for while in the city council,
although he had ample opportunity
to champion the cause of labor he
never showed any initiative in that
respect, and, while can not say
that he ever showed any hostility
toward labor. his lukewarm sup
port was such as one would expect
from any politician who was look
ing to his political future.


And after a careful consideration
Of his platform fail to see in 1t
anything of interest to the worker.


He promises in his platform
some things that are in force al
ready, such as Sunday closing OF
saloons and free employment Of
fee, which latter. by the way, is of
little service to the worker. as the
private employment offices have
almost monopoly of the work, ex-
cepting short jobs which the pri-
vate Offices do not care to bother
with.


On other points OF his platform
he is very indefinite. He speaks of
a concrete bridge at Monroe street,
but does not say II it Is to be built
by contract or day labor.


II Il was to be built by day labor
Is 1t not reasonable to suppose that
he would say so? The East
Sprague flll Is a fair sample of
what benefit to home labor the con-
tract system Of doing public work
is; where until recently word Of
English was seldom heard.


II Is true that he says In his plat
form that he is for home labor on
all public work, but surely he will
not pretend that he Is unaware OF
the fact that the corporation coun-
sci has held that such a provision
can not legally be Incorporated in
a contract.


He also says that no franchise Is
to be granted without sufficient
remuneration" to the city. Anoth-
er very indefinite statement. Who
p is to be the judge of what consti~
tutes a Sufficient remuneration"


Is Mr.. ONO and the company re-
ceiving the franchise to determine
that between themselves?


AS to another point in his plat.
form, the removal Of all poles with
in the fire limits, he does not say,
when this great reform Is to be ac-
complished, nor show In what re-
spect this masterpiece of legisla-
ton is of vital interest to the
worker.


] wish to speak of one more
point In his platform--the free pub
lic'baths. wonder II that would
be farce like the free employ
ment Office, or does he think the la
borer is In such desperate need of
an application of soap and water
that he can make 2 winning CAM
baign on such an Issue?


In fact my opinion is that his
best excuse for his candidacy
would be frank admission on his
part that he is at present out of Of
Ace and wants to be in, and since
his ward repudiated him as a coun-
Gilman he has determined that the
only way back to Office is by way
of the mayors office, Respectfully,


JAMES l.. WALTON,
Member of International Building
= Laborer's Union, Local 22s.
